content.full_text:
JAY ROBBINS
1237 8th Street
Imperial Beach, CA 91932
;,,,, 9?
March 20, 1992
Fair Political Practices Commission
-kgal Department
428 "J" Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, CA 95814
Dear Sirs:
An issue has been raised as to a potential conflict of interest I may have with regard to an
upcoming vote on temporary zoning code amendments proposed to be enacted pursuant to
a litigation settlement agreement. I would like to request formal written advice pursuant to
Government Code section 83114 (b) from the Fair Political Practices Commission.
I have a 1/3 interest in the proceeds of a promissory note secured by a deed of trust on a
property located in the SP-l (Specific Plan) Zone. I inherited this interest through my
father's estate. As of December 3L, 1.991, there was an outstanding balance on the
promissory note of $72,924.92. I received a total of $1,566.46 last year as my share of the
proceeds. I estimate the current value of the property to be from $250,000 to $350,000.
On April 17, 1991,, a citizens' group filed a lawsuit against the City of Imperial Beach
alleging various deficiencies of the General Plan, including the fact that the City had not
revised its Housing Element since 1980. The City was in the process of revising the element,
but it was not quite finished. After lengthy settlement negotiations, a settlement agreement
was entered into by the City Council on August 9, 7991. The terms of the settlement
agreement provided that the City would either enact temporary zoning amendments which
would affect City's multi-unit zones, or a judgment halting all development could be entered
against the City. Attached is a chart showing the changes that would take place if the
zoning amendments were passed.
I am unaware of any possible way that I may affect the value of the promissory note by
virtue of my vote on the proposed legislation.


FPPC
March 20,7992
Page Two
The City of Imperial Beach has a population of 26,645 people, according to 1990 census
figures. There are approximately 33.5 acres of SP-l zoned property in the City. The total
area of the City is 2,863 acres, of which 246 acres is a military airfield and 902 acres is an
open spaceArildlife presewe. Thus the SP-l district constitutes 1.95Vo of the City, if the
military airfield and the preserve is deleted. The Assessor's information indicates that there
are 250 properties and 394 dwelling units in the SP-1 zone.
This issue was to be heard and decided on March 1.8, 1992. It was continued to April 1,
1992, so that any conflict of interest issues could be determined. As I understand it, time
is of the essence because the court could be persuaded to enter judgment against the City
of Imperial Beach should it not enact the zoning code amendments soon. It is also my
understanding that in order for the amendments to become final, they must be reviewed by
the Coastal Commission, which, unless there is further delay, is scheduled for May, 1992.
I request that you respond as quickly as possible. Thank you for your consideration.
Jay Robbins
C.ouncilmember, City of Imperial Beach
JR:lb


EFFECT OF PROPOSED ZONING AMENDMENTS
The proposed zoning amendments would make the following changes in density:

In each of the above zones, the following limitation on lot consolidations is also proposed:
No lots which are subject to this Chapter and which are combined or
consolidated into one lot pursuant to the Subdivision Map Act or otherwise
shall be allowed to yield a greater density of residential units than such lots
would have yielded prior to the combining of said lots. This provision shall
not apply when the resulting lot yields two or fewer residential units. This
provision is void after January 7,7993.
All amendments would sunset on January L, L993,
J
3
3

sections.question: An issue has been raised as to a potential conflict of interest I may have with regard to an upcoming vote on temporary zoning code amendments proposed to be enacted pursuant to a litigation settlement agreement. I would like to request formal written advice pursuant to Government Code section 83114 (b) from the Fair Political Practices Commission.
sections.question_synthetic: Does Jay Robbins, a City Council member, have a conflict of interest in voting on proposed zoning code amendments when he holds a 1/3 interest in a promissory note secured by a deed of trust on property in the SP-1 zone that could be affected by the amendments?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a public official seeking FPPC advice, not an FPPC response. No formal CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER sections are present as this is the initial request. Document includes detailed attachments showing proposed zoning amendment changes. Confidence is high for document classification, though no FPPC response/analysis is included.

embedding.summary: Jay Robbins, a councilmember of Imperial Beach, requests formal FPPC advice regarding a potential conflict of interest in voting on temporary zoning code amendments that could affect property in the SP-1 zone in which he holds a 1/3 interest in a promissory note (worth approximately $72,924.92 with $1,566.46 annual proceeds). Robbins asserts he is unaware of any way his vote could affect the promissory note's value and provides detailed information about the property, city demographics, and the litigation settlement requiring the amendments.
